steps.title: Atmosphere Primed
narrative: Warm, moist air is surging northward across the Plains.
This step uses TV-style temperature markers and keeps the bottom scrubber synced to the METAR timeline.
Use the METAR controls to switch between temperature, dew point, wind, and pressure.

A.title: Atmosphere Primed
narrative: Warm, moist air is surging northward across the Plains.
This step uses TV-style temperature markers and keeps the bottom scrubber synced to the METAR timeline.
Use the METAR controls to switch between temperature, dew point, wind, and pressure.
A.videoNote: Everyone begins with Observe before going deeper.

B.title: Atmosphere Primed
narrative: Warm and humid air is moving northward into the region.
That moisture helps prepare the atmosphere for stronger storms later in the day.
B.videoNote: Use this level to connect the image to a science concept.

C.title: Atmosphere Primed
narrative: Meteorologists compare temperature, dew point, and wind together to judge how supportive the atmosphere is.
This is one of the first clues that the severe threat could increase later in the day.
C.videoNote: Best for older students or groups ready for more meteorology.

D.title: Atmosphere Primed
narrative: Where do you see the best overlap of warmth and moisture?
Which map product would you compare next to build confidence in the forecast?
D.videoNote: Use this level for forecasting questions, decision-making, or recap.

steps.title: Moisture Returns
narrative: This view switches the METAR markers to dew point so moisture return is easier to explain.
The clock and scrubber stay visible here too.

A.title: Moisture Returns
narrative: This view switches the METAR markers to dew point so moisture return is easier to explain.
The clock and scrubber stay visible here too.
A.videoNote: Everyone begins with Observe before going deeper.

B.title: Moisture Returns
narrative: Dew point helps show where richer low-level moisture is pooling.
Higher dew points often mark air that can help fuel stronger storms.
B.videoNote: Use this level to connect the image to a science concept.

C.title: Moisture Returns
narrative: A broader area of higher dew points usually means the atmosphere is becoming more supportive of instability.
This step helps students connect moisture to later storm intensity.
C.videoNote: Best for older students or groups ready for more meteorology.

D.title: Moisture Returns
narrative: Where would you expect the storm threat to increase first based on moisture?
How would you explain dew point to a younger class?
D.videoNote: Use this level for forecasting questions, decision-making, or recap.

steps.title: SPC Upgrade
narrative: The Storm Prediction Center highlights the region for organized severe weather.
Forecasters are increasingly concerned about widespread damaging winds.

A.title: SPC Upgrade
narrative: The Storm Prediction Center highlights the region for organized severe weather.
Forecasters are increasingly concerned about widespread damaging winds.
A.videoNote: Everyone begins with Observe before going deeper.

B.title: SPC Upgrade
narrative: The SPC outlook highlights where forecasters think severe storms are most likely.
A stronger category means confidence is increasing.
B.videoNote: Use this level to connect the image to a science concept.

C.title: SPC Upgrade
narrative: This outlook combines many ingredients: moisture, instability, wind shear, and forcing.
It is a forecast summary of how the atmosphere is expected to behave.
C.videoNote: Best for older students or groups ready for more meteorology.

D.title: SPC Upgrade
narrative: What would make you more concerned after seeing this outlook?
What would you compare next on the map to test the forecast idea?
D.videoNote: Use this level for forecasting questions, decision-making, or recap.

steps.title: Forecast Temperatures
narrative: Future temperatures should scrub by HRRR frames, matching the way future winds behaves.
State boundaries and county lines remain on for context.

A.title: Forecast Temperatures
narrative: Future temperatures should scrub by HRRR frames, matching the way future winds behaves.
State boundaries and county lines remain on for context.
A.videoNote: Everyone begins with Observe before going deeper.

B.title: Forecast Temperatures
narrative: Explain what is happening.
B.videoNote: Use this level to connect the image to a science concept.

C.title: Forecast Temperatures
narrative: Analyze the science.
C.videoNote: Best for older students or groups ready for more meteorology.

D.title: Forecast Temperatures
narrative: Apply and forecast.
D.videoNote: Use this level for forecasting questions, decision-making, or recap.

steps.title: Instability Builds
narrative: If a CAPE manifest is present, this step will show it.
If not, this step can be skipped without breaking the demo.

A.title: Instability Builds
narrative: If a CAPE manifest is present, this step will show it.
If not, this step can be skipped without breaking the demo.

D.title: Instability Builds

steps.title: Wind Energy Builds
narrative: Forecast wind fields show strong flow feeding the developing system.
This step remains on the HRRR frame scrubber path.

steps.title: Storms Begin to Develop
narrative: Initial storms begin to organize into a more connected line.
Radar is now the best tool for tracking rapid evolution.
